name: panda-common
documentation:
# Panda Common Project

Includes all libraries used by PanDA server, JEDI, Harvester, monitor and others.

modules.name: pandacommon.kafkapublisher.KafkaPublisher
documentation:
Module for sending messages to Kafka that will be used
by the panda server and JEDI to publish task/job state transitions.
pandamon will consume those messages for realtime logging.

Logger and monitoring data collector for Panda.
Custom fields added to the logging:

user     Who is running the app
PandaID  Panda job ID (if applicable)
ID       General usage ID (eg. pilot ID, scheduler ID). A string.
type     Message type

methods.name: pandacommon.pandamsgbkr.msg_processor.SimpleMsgProcPluginBase.get_pid
documentation: get generic pid, including hostname, os process id, thread id

Base class of simple message processor plugin
Simple message processor suits following cases:
    - one-out: to create a messages and send them to one queue
    - one-in: to receive messages from one queue and processes the messages
    - one-in-one-out: to receive messages from one queue, processes the messages to create new messages, and then and send new messages to another queue
simple message processor plugin Base

modules.name: pandacommon.liveconfigparser.LiveConfigParser
documentation: Module to be/wrap a ConfigParser instance, providing simple access via standard Python member
and Dictionary interfaces. This is purely for convenience
[default]
key1=value1
key2=value2
[section1]
key3=val3
key4=val4
A LiveConfigParser object lets you access these via
c.default['key1'] -> value1
c.section1['key3'] -> val3

functions.name: pandacommon.pandautils.PandaUtils.batched
documentation:
Batch data from the iterable into tuples of length n. The last batch may be shorter than n
If strict is true, will raise a ValueError if the final batch is shorter than n
Note this function is for Python <= 3.11 as it mimics itertools.batched() in Python 3.13

functions.name: pandacommon.pandautils.PandaUtils.get_sql_IN_bind_variables
documentation:
Get the comma-separated string expression with bind variables to be used with SQL IN-condition and the corresponding variable map
E.g. get_sql_IN_bind_variables(["done", "finished", "aborted"], prefix=":status_") will return the tuple:
     (':status_1,:status_2,:status_3', {':status_1': 'done', ':status_2': 'finished', ':status_3': 'aborted'})

Args:
    values (iterable): list or other iterables of the values
    prefix (str): prefix of variable name 

Returns:
    str: comma-separated string of variable names of all bind variables, to be put inside the parentheses of SQL IN (...)
    dict: map of variable names and values, to be put as variable map of SQL execute
